summary:
The El Omari culture was a Lower Egyptian Predynastic culture located near the site of El Omari (modern Helwan), south of Cairo, dating to approximately 4600–4400 BCE. Positioned chronologically and culturally between the earlier [[event:1534|Merimde Beni-Salama]] tradition and the contemporaneous Badarian culture of Upper Egypt, El Omari provides evidence for a distinct farming community in the Nile Valley near the Delta-Valley boundary.

El Omari communities cultivated emmer wheat and barley and kept cattle, sheep, and goats. Their material culture included simple, relatively undecorated pottery, polished stone tools, and bone implements. Burials found at the site were shallow pit graves with the dead interred in a contracted position, often with simple grave goods including pottery and food offerings. The absence of elaborate grave goods compared to contemporaneous Upper Egyptian cultures suggests a relatively egalitarian social structure.

The El Omari culture represents one thread in the complex tapestry of Predynastic Egyptian cultural development, demonstrating that the transition to farming in the Nile Valley involved distinct regional traditions adapting the Neolithic package to local environmental conditions before the eventual cultural unification that preceded Pharaonic state formation.

read_more_links.title: Neolithic Nile Valley - World History Encyclopedia
